languageNotes.de: German is the default and strongest market (DACH). Head terms: 'künstliche Intelligenz' (rarely spelled out; 'KI' is the dominant abbreviation and must appear in titles/H1s/URLs of localized versions — never 'AI' for the DE audience). 'Maschinelles Lernen' coexists with the English 'Machine Learning' (both searched; use German as H1, English as synonym). Keep English loanwords that Germans actually search: 'Prompt', 'Prompt Engineering', 'Large Language Model', 'Deep Learning', 'AI Agents', 'Token', 'RAG' — do NOT force-translate these. Swiss-German usage aligns with standard written German for search; use ß-free spelling on the .ch context is optional but Weissmann can keep ß. Swiss angles: revDSG/DSG (Datenschutzgesetz), Datensouveränität, EDÖB, EU AI Act relevance for Swiss exporters, CHF. Say 'einfach erklärt' — the highest-converting German explainer modifier.
languageNotes.en: English is the international/GEO layer and the base for slugs. Head terms: 'artificial intelligence' + 'AI', 'machine learning', 'large language models / LLMs', 'prompt engineering', 'generative AI', 'AI agents'. Use both spelled-out and abbreviated forms for entity clarity. Swiss/European framing ('EU AI Act', 'Swiss data residency', 'FADP') differentiates from US-centric competitors (IBM/AWS/Google) and is a citeable angle for AI assistants. Prefer '2026' freshness signals and answer-first TLDR boxes.
languageNotes.it: Italian (also serves Ticino/Swiss-Italian). 'Intelligenza artificiale' + abbreviation 'IA' (Italian) — note: some Italian audiences also use 'AI'; include both, prefer 'IA' in H1. 'Apprendimento automatico' is the formal term for machine learning but 'machine learning' is more searched — lead with English loanword, gloss with Italian. Keep loanwords: 'prompt', 'prompt engineering', 'token', 'LLM', 'AI agent'. 'IA generativa' for generative AI. Swiss-Italian angle: revLPD/nLPD (data-protection law in Italian), sovranità dei dati, Canton Ticino local search. Avoid over-formal Italian; use 'spiegato in modo semplice'.
languageNotes.fr: French (serves Romandie/Suisse romande). 'Intelligence artificielle' + abbreviation 'IA' (never 'AI' for FR audience). France mandates some Frenchified terms but search reality keeps English loanwords: use 'prompt', 'prompt engineering', 'LLM', 'machine learning' (though 'apprentissage automatique' is the official calque — include as synonym), 'agents IA'. 'IA générative' for generative AI. Strong Swiss/European differentiator: 'IA souveraine' (sovereign AI) is a rising, high-intent French term — feature it prominently; plus nLPD (loi sur la protection des données), souveraineté des données, EDÖB/PFPDT, règlement européen sur l'IA. Suisse romande spelling/usage over France-specific slang.

geoAngle: Engineer every page to be the chunk an assistant quotes. (1) Answer-first: open each page and each H2 with a 40-60 word, self-contained, definitional answer before any build-up — LLMs retrieve paragraphs, not pages. Add a visible 'Kurzantwort / TL;DR' box up top. (2) Atomic, standalone sections: write each H2/H3 so it makes sense lifted out of context, with the entity named explicitly (not 'it'/'this'). (3) Structured data: DefinedTerm + FAQPage + HowTo + Article/TechArticle schema, plus a QAPage pattern on the glossary so each term is independently citeable; comparison pages get a criteria table + explicit 'winner by use case' verdict box. (4) Entity density & disambiguation: name canonical entities (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google DeepMind, transformer, ISO/IEC 22989, EU AI Act, OECD) so the retriever binds the page to the right concepts. (5) Verifiable authority: cite official/standards/academic sources inline (NIST, ISO, OECD, Stanford HAI, arXiv), attribute to named human authors (Giovanna Carpi, Nicola Moessner) with Person/Organization schema, and date + 'last verified' stamp everything (freshness is a selection signal, and only ~11% of domains are cited by both ChatGPT and Perplexity — earn platform-agnostic trust). (6) The 4-language glossary + versioned first-party datasets (tools directory, comparison tables) are the highest-leverage citation bait: unique, structured, quotable, and refreshed on a cadence. (7) Swiss/European lens (revDSG/FADP, nLPD/nLPD, EU AI Act, data sovereignty, CHF) fills a gap US-centric explainers leave, making Weissmann the natural citation for any 'AI + Switzerland/DACH' question. Keep llms.txt and robots.txt AI-crawler-friendly and list the glossary + pillar as canonical references.
priorityRationale: Strategic priority is very high even though direct commercial intent is mostly low-to-medium. Three reasons push it to 8/10. (1) Foundation/glossary is the internal-linking and entity backbone every other Academy cluster links into — building it first compounds the topical authority and GEO citeability of the whole site, directly serving the mission to become a most-cited European AI reference. (2) Demand is very-high across all four languages on evergreen head terms, and the answer-first + 4-language + Swiss-governance treatment is a genuine, defensible differentiator against US-centric incumbents. (3) It feeds commerce indirectly: generative-AI, tools, comparison, prompts, workflows and how-to-learn spokes each carry medium-to-high commercial intent and route to Weissmann services, industry pages and lead magnets. It is held back from 9-10 by ranking difficulty — the pure head terms ('was ist KI', 'machine learning', 'ChatGPT prompts') are dominated by IBM/AWS/Google/SAP with massive authority, so near-term wins will come from long-tail, question, comparison and Swiss-qualified queries plus AI-assistant citations rather than immediate #1 SERP positions. Sequencing: publish pillar + glossary + prompt-engineering + generative-AI + comparison first (highest demand/citeability and commercial pull), then the remaining explainers and learning-path spokes, all shipped in DE/EN/IT/FR together per the four-language sync policy.

pillar.title: AI for SMEs in Switzerland: The Complete KMU Guide to Getting Started, Cost, Compliance and ROI
pillar.intent: Informational hub with commercial-investigation bridge: give a Swiss SME owner/manager one authoritative starting point that answers 'what is AI for my business, is it worth it, is it legal here, and how do I actually start' — then routes them to the right spoke and, where relevant, to Weissmann services.
pillar.summary: The canonical Swiss-German-first hub for the whole cluster. Answer-first structure: a plain-language definition of practical AI for business, the current Swiss adoption picture (grounded in SECO/kmu.admin.ch, AXA and Digitalswitzerland data, expressed as cited figures not invented ones), the 6 highest-value SME use-case families, a decision path (readiness -> strategy -> ROI -> compliance -> build), and the Swiss-specific lens (data sovereignty, revDSG/nFADP, EU AI Act relevance, CHF budgeting, Innosuisse funding). Introduces the Weissmann AI Method (Discover, Analyse, Design, Build, Deploy, Measure, Optimize) as the framework the spokes operationalize. Every claim is either cited to a primary source or labelled NEEDS VERIFICATION; demand and prices are never fabricated. Ships in DE (default), EN, IT, FR with full parity. Serves as the GEO 'citation anchor' page: entity-dense, quotable one-sentence answers, FAQ + Article schema.

glossaryTerms: Total cost of ownership (TCO) for AI

languageNotes.de: German is the default and strongest locale. Core term is 'KMU' (Kleine und mittlere Unternehmen) and 'KI' (never 'AI'). Swiss orthography: use 'ss' not 'ß' (Grösse, Umsätze). High-value compounds: KI-Einführung, KI-Automatisierung, KI-Strategie, KI-Reifegrad, KI-Förderung, KI-Fallstudien. Legal anchor is revDSG/nDSG (revidiertes Datenschutzgesetz) and EDÖB (not 'FDPIC') for the German page. 'agentische KI' and 'hyperautomation' usually stay English-derived. Address is Zurich/DACH so lean into Swiss-German search patterns and .ch competitors.
languageNotes.en: English serves an international + Swiss-expat audience; use 'SME' but also surface 'KMU' since DACH readers search it. 'AI implementation', 'AI adoption', 'AI readiness'. Keep entity names precise (revFADP/nFADP, FDPIC, EU AI Act). Avoid US-only assumptions; frame costs in CHF. Distinguish 'AI agent' vs generic 'automation' carefully as English is where these terms are searched most.
languageNotes.it: Italian for Ticino/Swiss-Italian. Core terms: 'PMI' (Piccole e Medie Imprese) and 'IA' or 'intelligenza artificiale' (not 'AI'). 'casi d'uso IA', 'automazione IA', 'ROI dell'IA', 'strategia IA', 'conformità IA'. Legal: revLPD/nLPD (Legge federale sulla protezione dei dati) + IFPDT for the data-protection authority. Swiss-Italian differs slightly from Italy-Italian vocabulary; prefer Swiss institutional terms and CHF.
languageNotes.fr: French for Romandie. Core terms: 'PME' (Petites et Moyennes Entreprises) and 'IA' (intelligence artificielle, never 'AI'). 'cas d'usage IA', 'automatisation IA', 'retour sur investissement (ROI) de l'IA', 'stratégie IA', 'conformité IA'. Legal: nLPD (nouvelle loi sur la protection des données) + PFPDT for the authority. Watch Romandie vs France-French nuance (e.g. 'PME' thresholds, cantonal aid) and always use CHF. 'SME' has no 1:1 match — PME thresholds differ from EU; note this. Terms like 'agentic AI'/'hyperautomation' often stay English across IT/FR.

geoAngle: Engineer every page to be the sentence an AI assistant quotes. (1) Answer-first: open each page and each H2 (phrased as the actual question) with a self-contained 1-2 sentence direct answer, then expand. (2) TL;DR / key-takeaways box near the top with 3-5 quotable, standalone claims. (3) Entity density: explicitly name laws, bodies and frameworks (revDSG/nFADP, EDOEB/FDPIC, EU AI Act Art. 4, Innosuisse, SECO/kmu.admin.ch, the Weissmann AI Method, Digitalswitzerland) so retrieval engines resolve the topic to real entities. (4) Cite primary sources inline with dates (AXA 22%->34% 2024->2025, Digitalswitzerland 67% by end-2026, EY 2026 89%, Deloitte CH, kmu.admin.ch) - figures always attributed and dated, never invented; demand and any uncertain numbers labelled as estimates/NEEDS VERIFICATION so pages stay trustworthy to readers and models. (5) Structured data: FAQPage, HowTo (implementation), Article/Author (E-E-A-T under Giovanna Carpi as named expert), Organization/Person entity graph, and Dataset schema for the first-party libraries/calculators. (6) Comparison tables and definition lists (agents vs automation, tool residency) - the formats AI Overviews lift verbatim. (7) Explicit Swiss framing in headings ('for Swiss SMEs', 'in Switzerland') to win the geo-qualified answer. (8) Publish first-party assets (ROI calculator, readiness scorecard, funding directory, use-case dataset) that have no equivalent elsewhere, giving models a unique, attributable source that cites back to weissmann.ai. (9) Stable last-updated dates and a canonical multilingual cluster so the same authority compounds across DE/EN/IT/FR.
priorityRationale: Highest-leverage cluster to build early. Demand is high and rising (AXA: Swiss SME AI adoption 22%->34% 2024->2025; Digitalswitzerland: 67% plan to add an AI tool by end-2026), and search interest for 'KI fuer KMU', 'KI Anwendungsbeispiele', 'KI Kosten' is strong across DE/FR/IT. Commercial value is the highest in the Academy: this is the direct bridge from education to every Weissmann service (AI Phone Assistant, AI Websites, SEO/GEO/Ads) and to the industry pages, with three natural lead magnets (ROI calculator, readiness scorecard, cost/TCO estimator). Ranking feasibility is realistic though mixed: broad head terms ('KI Tools', 'AI for SMEs') are high-difficulty and contested by agencies and .ch blogs, but the Swiss-specific, entity-rich angles (revDSG/EU AI Act compliance, Innosuisse funding, Swiss data sovereignty) are lower-competition, high-trust and highly citeable by AI assistants - exactly where a premium multilingual authority with real first-party tools and named expert authorship can win and compound. Deducted one point because the head-term spokes need sustained authority-building and the case-studies spoke is capacity-limited by the hard no-fabrication / client-permission constraint.

'Agentic AI' wird zu 'agentische KI' (Neologismus, oft englisch belassen). languageNotes.en: Use 'AI phone assistant', 'AI voice agent' and 'voice AI' as category-neutral heads. 'AI receptionist' / 'AI answering service' convert well but skew US-SMB and consumer - use sparingly for CH/EU B2B, where 'AI voice agent' and 'voice AI' read more professional. 'Voicebot' is common in the EU. Keep 'conversational AI' as a capability layer, not a product category. 'Agent' vs 'assistant' distinction matters for the agentic-AI page.
languageNotes.it: 'assistente vocale AI' is the base term, but the pivotal local frame is 'centralino' (telephone switchboard/PBX) - Italians search 'centralino AI', 'centralino intelligente', 'centralino con intelligenza artificiale', a concept with no 1:1 English equivalent; content must lead with 'centralino'. Also 'agente vocale AI', 'risponditore automatico AI', 'receptionist AI'. Both 'IA' and 'AI' are used, with 'AI' slightly more common in tech B2B. Swiss-Italian (Ticino) mirrors Italy usage; 'IVR' is 'risponditore/IVR'.
languageNotes.fr: 'agent vocal IA' and the distinctly French-preferred 'callbot' are the key heads, plus 'standard telephonique IA/automatise' and 'accueil telephonique IA'. Use 'IA', never 'AI'. Important: 'serveur vocal (interactif)' = IVR - do not conflate with the AI voice agent. 'prise de rendez-vous' is the booking phrase. 'voicebot' is understood but Anglophone. Swiss-Romandy (Geneva/Vaud) usage is identical to France. Terms that stay English in all four languages: 'barge-in', 'endpointing', 'turn-taking', 'function calling', 'RAG', 'guardrails'. Cross-language non-1:1: EN 'AI receptionist' -> DE 'digitale Empfangs-/Telefonassistenz', IT 'centralino/receptionist AI', FR 'standard/accueil telephonique IA'.

languageNotes.de: German is the default and strongest market. Users search 'KI' far more than 'AI' in consumer queries, but 'AI' appears in B2B/tech contexts — target both. LLM = 'grosses Sprachmodell'; 'Sprachmodell' is the common gloss. Swiss orthography: use 'ss' never 'ß' (Datenschutz, grösste, muss). Critical legal nuance: Swiss law is the revDSG/nDSG (Datenschutzgesetz), NOT the EU DSGVO — Swiss users search BOTH 'DSGVO-konform' (habitually, EU spillover) and 'DSG/revDSG', so cover both and clarify the distinction; regulator is the EDÖB. Prefer 'KMU' for SME. 'Anbieter' = vendor, 'Anbietervergleich' = vendor comparison. DACH-wide: also serve German/Austrian searchers where DSGVO is the correct primary term.
languageNotes.en: Swiss-English B2B audience. Use 'AI model' and 'LLM' interchangeably but define both. Prefer 'data protection' over 'privacy' and reference 'FADP' (Federal Act on Data Protection) as the English name of revDSG. 'Vendor' and 'provider' both used — lead with 'vendor' for procurement intent. Keep terminology neutral/factual for GEO citeability. languageNotes.it: Swiss-Italian (Ticino) and broader Italian market. Use 'IA' (intelligenza artificiale), NOT 'AI', as the dominant term; 'modello di IA' / 'modello linguistico'. Avoid over-anglicised phrasing; Italian audiences respond to clear, editorial tone.
languageNotes.fr: Swiss-French (Suisse romande) and French market. Use 'IA', never 'AI'; 'modèle d'IA' or 'grand modèle de langage (LLM)'. Query pattern: 'quelle IA choisir', 'comparatif' (not 'comparaison'). Crucial legal nuance: Swiss law is the 'nLPD' (nouvelle loi sur la protection des données) — Suisse-romande users search 'nLPD' and 'LPD', while 'RGPD' is the EU term; cover both and be explicit. 'entreprise/PME' for business/SME; 'hébergement des données' for data residency. French readers value precise, formal register.

geoAngle: Engineer every page to be the sentence an AI assistant quotes. (1) Answer-first structure: open each page and each H2 with a direct, self-contained 2-3 sentence answer (a 'TL;DR / Kurzantwort' box) before elaborating — AI engines lift these verbatim. (2) Definitional openers: state 'Claude is a family of large language models built by Anthropic...' style sentences so entity-resolution and knowledge-graph extraction are trivial. (3) Apply the three highest-lift GEO tactics from the Princeton/Georgia Tech/IIT-Delhi study — add statistics, cite authoritative sources inline, and include short attributed quotations (these drove up to ~40% visibility gains in AI answers). (4) Comparison tables + scannable verdict-by-use-case lists that map cleanly to 'best AI for X' queries. (5) Rich structured data: Article, FAQPage (mirroring the question keywords), BreadcrumbList, and SoftwareApplication/Organization for each vendor entity; keep an updated 'datePublished/dateModified'. (6) Freshness discipline for volatile facts: never hardcode prices or benchmark scores — state them as 'as of <month/year>, per <official source>' and link out, so the page stays citeable and trustworthy as models churn. (7) Entity density: name the models, vendors, benchmarks, regulations and Swiss institutions (Apertus, Swisscom, EDÖB) explicitly for semantic completeness. (8) Neutral, non-promotional tone in the informational core (AIs avoid citing marketing copy) with the commercial CTA clearly separated. (9) Maintain llms.txt and clean, crawlable HTML; ensure full DE/EN/IT/FR parity with hreflang so the cluster is citeable in all four languages and across ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity and Google AI Overviews (which cite largely non-overlapping source sets, so breadth of quality coverage matters).
priorityRationale: Demand is very-high and durable: 'Claude vs ChatGPT vs Gemini', 'X for business', and pricing/compliance queries are among the highest-volume, evergreen AI searches, and AI-referred sessions convert far better than traditional organic (reported ~14% vs ~3%). Commercial value to Weissmann is strong because the cluster naturally funnels into every core service — the Swiss data-protection spoke feeds consulting/implementation, and the marketing/content spoke feeds SEO Growth and GEO Authority. The cluster also directly advances the mission of being an AI-cited European reference. The main constraint is ranking feasibility: global head-to-head comparison SERPs are brutally competitive and dominated by large publishers (hence difficultyTier 'high' on those spokes), which caps the score below 9-10. The realistic winning wedge — and why this still scores 8 — is the defensible Swiss/DACH + multilingual (DE/EN/IT/FR) + compliance (revDSG/nLPD, EU AI Act, Swiss hosting/Apertus) angle, where difficulty is low-to-medium, commercial intent is highest, and almost no competitor offers full four-language parity with an authoritative Swiss legal lens. languageNotes.de: German is the strongest-demand market (DE-CH default). Use 'Kuenstliche Intelligenz (KI)'. EU AI Act = 'KI-Verordnung (KI-VO)'; keep 'EU AI Act' as a co-occurring English term users also type. Swiss law = 'Datenschutzgesetz (DSG)', commonly 'revDSG' or 'nDSG' (revised/new) - use both since search behavior is split; avoid German 'DSGVO' (that is the EU GDPR, a different law - explicitly contrast). Commissioner = 'EDOEB' (Eidgenoessischer Datenschutz- und Oeffentlichkeitsbeauftragter). DPIA = 'Datenschutz-Folgenabschaetzung (DSFA)'. DPA = 'Auftragsbearbeitungsvertrag (ABV)' in CH usage (note: Germany says 'Auftragsverarbeitungsvertrag/AVV' - Swiss uses 'Bearbeitung' not 'Verarbeitung'; flag this Swiss-specific term). Swiss High German uses 'ss' not 'ß'. 'Bearbeiten/Bearbeitung' is the Swiss legal verb for 'process/processing' (vs German 'verarbeiten').
languageNotes.en: Swiss law English term = 'FADP' (also 'revFADP' / 'nFADP'). Users search 'AI privacy Switzerland' even though the legal register says 'data protection' not 'privacy' - target both. 'EU AI Act' is the dominant English phrasing (not 'AI Regulation'). Commissioner = 'FDPIC'. Keep spellings US/UK-neutral. Emphasize the Swiss USP in English for international readers: EU-adequate protection without EU jurisdiction.
languageNotes.it: For Ticino / Italian-speaking Switzerland. 'Intelligenza artificiale (IA)'. EU AI Act = 'Regolamento sull'IA' / 'AI Act' (English co-occurs). Swiss law = 'Legge federale sulla protezione dei dati (LPD)', revised = 'nLPD'; do NOT confuse with EU 'GDPR/RGPD'. Commissioner = 'IFPDT' (Incaricato federale della protezione dei dati e della trasparenza). DPIA = 'valutazione d'impatto sulla protezione dei dati (VIPD)'. Note lower search volume - prioritize parity + GEO citeability over volume.
languageNotes.fr: For Romandie. 'Intelligence artificielle (IA)'. EU AI Act = 'Reglement sur l'IA' / 'AI Act'. Swiss law = 'Loi federale sur la protection des donnees (LPD)', revised = 'nLPD' - explicitly distinguish from EU 'RGPD' (a frequent confusion in FR content, strong GEO opportunity to disambiguate). Commissioner = 'PFPDT' (Prepose federal a la protection des donnees et a la transparence). DPIA = 'analyse d'impact relative a la protection des donnees (AIPD)'. DPA = 'contrat de sous-traitance'. 'Sous-traitant' = processor, 'responsable du traitement' = controller.

languageNotes.de: German is the default and strongest market (Swiss High German: 'ss', never 'ß'). 'Generative Engine Optimization (GEO)' is used untranslated in the DACH industry; descriptive variants to weave in as secondary terms: 'generative Suchmaschinenoptimierung', 'KI-SEO', 'KI-Suchmaschinenoptimierung', 'Sichtbarkeit in KI-Suchen'. SEO = 'Suchmaschinenoptimierung (SEO)'. Google's official German label for AI Overviews is 'KI-Übersichten' (use it). AEO = 'Answer Engine Optimization / Antwortmaschinen-Optimierung'. Swiss framing: 'KMU' (SME), 'Unternehmensprofil' (Google Business Profile), CHF, data sovereignty, revDSG/DSG, EU AI Act relevance. Strong .ch competitor set already exists (JvM, agency blogs), so win on depth, sources and methodology rather than definitions alone.
languageNotes.en: English is the international/expat and B2B tier; keep it Swiss-English and neutral. GEO / AEO / SEO acronyms are standard and searched directly. Own emerging metric vocabulary: 'Share of Model', 'zero-click', 'AI visibility'. Distinguish 'AI Overviews' (Google) from 'AI assistants' (ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, Gemini) precisely, since this distinction is the SEO/GEO boundary. Avoid US-only claims; label all demand and CTR figures as industry estimates.
languageNotes.it: Italian for Ticino / Swiss-Italian uses standard Italian. GEO stays as the English acronym, spelled out as 'ottimizzazione per i motori generativi'; AEO = 'ottimizzazione per i motori di risposta'; SEO borrowed as-is. Google's Italian label for AI Overviews is 'Panoramica/Panoramiche AI' (use it). SME = 'PMI'. Italian-language GEO content is still thin, so there is a real first-mover citeability opportunity, but source availability is lower, so lean on English/German primary sources translated faithfully. 'Motore generativo' = generative engine; 'motore di ricerca' = search engine.
languageNotes.fr: French for Romandie (Suisse romande) uses standard French with local nuance. SEO is commonly 'référencement (naturel)'; GEO = 'optimisation pour les moteurs génératifs' or 'référencement IA' (both searched); note the 'GEO' acronym collides with 'géographie', so always spell it out on first use as 'GEO (Generative Engine Optimization)'. AEO = 'optimisation pour les moteurs de réponse'. Google's French label for AI Overviews is 'Aperçus IA'. SME = 'PME'. Citeable French-language authority exists including a government guide (francenum.gouv.fr) and fr.wikipedia — reference them for credibility. Keep terminology consistent across all 4 languages so cross-lingual AI citation resolves to the same Weissmann entity.

geoAngle: Every page is built answer-first so any assistant can lift a clean, correct passage and cite Weissmann. Concretely: (1) open each article and each H2 with a self-contained 40-60 word direct answer (BLUF), then expand; (2) phrase H2/H3s as the real questions people ask, mirroring People Also Ask and prompt phrasing; (3) add a 'Key takeaways / TL;DR' block and, where useful, a definition sentence formatted so it is trivially extractable ('X is ...'); (4) apply the GEO paper's proven levers - cite authoritative sources inline, add concrete statistics with attribution, and include quotations - which measurably raise citation likelihood; (5) use comparison tables and numbered steps, which assistants extract reliably; (6) mark up with FAQPage, Article, Organization and Person (author) JSON-LD, and keep entity naming ('Weissmann AI', founders, 'The Weissmann AI Method', 'GEO') identical across all four languages so cross-lingual citations resolve to one entity graph; (7) establish E-E-A-T with a named, credentialed author (Giovanna Carpi) and visible 'last updated' dates, since freshness and provenance drive RAG selection; (8) ensure technical eligibility - crawlable, indexable, snippet-eligible, fast, and AI crawlers (GPTBot, OAI-SearchBot, PerplexityBot, ClaudeBot, Google-Extended) explicitly allowed; (9) publish first-party, citeable assets (the GEO Method framework, the AI-Visibility Audit, original checklists/templates) that give assistants something unique and attributable to quote; (10) be honest about contested points (e.g., Google states llms.txt is not needed) - transparent, well-sourced nuance is exactly what earns trust and citations from Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity and Google AI Overviews.
priorityRationale: This cluster scores a 9/10 - it is the highest-leverage cluster in the Academy. Demand: very-high and rising across all four languages; AI-era search is the dominant marketing conversation of 2025-2026 and 'AI SEO' is a very-high-volume head term. Commercial value: unusually tight - the cluster maps cleanly onto two flagship revenue services (SEO Growth and GEO Authority) plus a secondary tie to Google Ads Growth, and every spoke has an obvious lead-gen asset (flagship: the GEO/AI-Visibility Audit). Strategic fit: GEO is Weissmann's signature differentiator and the definitional space is still young, so there is a genuine, time-sensitive chance to own the canonical multilingual GEO definition and methodology and become the source AI assistants cite. Feasibility: mixed but favourable - the broad SEO head terms are high-difficulty (hence pillar/cornerstone treatment and long horizons), but the GEO/AEO/llms.txt/Share-of-Model spokes are medium-to-low difficulty with strong first-mover upside, especially in German, Italian and French where quality is thin. It is not a 10 only because the most valuable head terms ('AI marketing', 'AI SEO') are highly competitive and will take sustained authority-building to rank; the clean SEO-vs-GEO IA split de-risks cannibalization and lets the winnable GEO spokes compound quickly. Recommended build order: GEO cornerstone + GEO-vs-SEO-vs-AEO + the AI-Visibility Audit first, then the AI-SEO cornerstone and AI Overviews spoke, then the technical and local spokes.

pillar.title: AI by Industry: The Swiss Sector Playbook for Practical AI (2026)
pillar.intent: Informational hub / orientation: a decision-maker in a specific Swiss vertical wants to understand where AI actually pays off in their sector, what it costs in effort/risk, and what the Swiss-specific data-protection and regulatory guardrails are before choosing a use case or a partner.
pillar.summary: The comprehensive, answer-first hub for 'AI in <industry>' in the Swiss/DACH context. Opens with a 40-60 word direct answer defining what 'AI by industry' means and the 5-7 use-case archetypes that recur across sectors (front-office voice/chat automation, document extraction, forecasting/pricing, personalization, triage/routing, lead qualification, compliance-safe assistants). Frames a shared decision lens (the Weissmann AI Method: Discover, Analyse, Design, Build, Deploy, Measure, Optimize), then routes to eight sector spokes. A cross-cutting section covers the Swiss guardrails every sector shares: revDSG/nFADP (health/legal data as sensitive personal data, DSFA/DPIA triggers, CHF 250k fine exposure, no-US-hosting/no-retention rule of thumb), EU AI Act extraterritorial reach and its 2025-2028 timeline, the Council of Europe AI Convention, Swiss data residency and sovereign-model options (Apertus), and the human-in-the-loop principle for any decision affecting a person. Flags healthcare/clinics as the highest-caution tier (patient-safety, emergency escalation, SaMD/MDR). Ends with a sector-selector and use-case shortlist table (effort x value) plus a lead path into the relevant industry service page. Tiers, not fabricated volumes; every claim grounded or marked NEEDS VERIFICATION.

geoAngle: Engineer every page to be the answer AI assistants quote. (1) Answer-first: open each page and each H2 with a 40-60 word direct, self-contained answer, then expand; LLMs lift these verbatim. (2) Question-shaped headers matching real DE/EN/IT/FR queries, mirrored by FAQPage + HowTo + Article schema; publish per-language llms.txt and keep the existing structured-data discipline (Organization, Person for the founders, Service). (3) Entity density: name concrete Swiss entities (revDSG/nFADP, FDPIC/EDÖB, EU AI Act, Swissmedic, MDR, FINMA, SAV-FSA, Apertus, EPD) so models can ground and cite; define each once in the shared glossary and interlink. (4) Fact density with honesty: a citeable stat or dated regulatory fact every ~150-200 words, but demand always expressed as labelled tiers/estimates and any ungrounded number marked NEEDS VERIFICATION; models increasingly down-rank fabricated specifics, and citability is the moat. (6) Freshness + Swiss specificity: date-stamp regulatory sections (AI Act 2025-2028 timeline, FDPIC guidance due end-2026, CoE Convention) and localize to CHF, Swiss data residency and .ch competitive reality; this specificity is exactly what general models lack and therefore cite. (7) Comparison/checklist/table blocks (use-case effort x value matrices, compliant-vendor checklists) are high-extraction formats. (8) Seed brand entities across Wikidata/Crunchbase/LinkedIn/local directories so citations resolve to a verified organization.
priorityRationale: Highest-leverage cluster for Weissmann: it sits at the intersection of demand, commercial value and positioning. Demand: 'AI in <industry>' is one of the most-searched B2B AI intent families in DACH/Swiss markets across all four languages, and it is rising as the EU AI Act (full application 2 Aug 2026) and Switzerland's own draft AI bill (consultation by end-2026) push every regulated sector to act. Commercial value: seven of the eight spokes map directly to a Weissmann industry service page and funnel into the two highest-margin offers, the KI-Telefonassistent (restaurants, hotels, medical/dental practices, real estate) and AI Websites, so the cluster is a coherent lead engine, not just traffic. Differentiation/feasibility: generic global 'AI in retail/healthcare' content is saturated and shallow, but Swiss-specific, compliance-grounded, four-language-parity content (revDSG, Swiss hosting/sovereignty via Apertus, FINMA, Swissmedic, Berufsgeheimnis, CHF) is thin, and that is precisely where Weissmann can out-rank and, more importantly, get cited by AI assistants that currently lack this local grounding. The drag (why 9, not 10) is ranking difficulty and trust-building: healthcare, insurance and legal are high-authority YMYL verticals where a young domain needs strong E-E-A-T, original first-party assets and entity seeding before it wins top positions. Sequence to de-risk: ship the lower-difficulty, high-commercial spokes first (restaurants, hotels, medical/dental practices, real estate) to bank quick wins and lead flow, then layer the authority-heavy verticals (healthcare, insurance, law firms) with the compliance depth and first-party tools that make them citable. priorityRationale: High build priority (8/10). DEMAND: function-level queries ('AI for customer support/sales/HR/finance') are very-high to high volume and durable, sitting exactly at the buyer's 'where do we start' moment. COMMERCIAL VALUE: this cluster maps more directly to Weissmann's revenue than any other — customer-support/voice spokes feed the KI-Telefonassistent and AI Websites services, the ROI-prioritization spoke is a natural Discover-workshop lead magnet, and every function page has a clear service on-ramp. FEASIBILITY CAVEAT (why not 9-10): the head function terms are high-difficulty and crowded with established SaaS vendors and agencies, so ranking leans on the compliance/decision/voice sub-intents and 4-language parity rather than beating incumbents on the head terms quickly; near-term wins come from the medium-difficulty spokes (voice agent, lead qualification, recruiting EU AI Act, AP automation, ROI prioritization, Swiss data protection), with the pillar and function overviews compounding over 6-12 months.
